citations.desc: Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC).
citations.narrative:
Review of IACUC records, in addition to interviews with facility personnel and IACUC members, identified the following
instances where laboratory staff implemented significant changes to ongoing protocols without prior review and approval
by the IACUC.
In February 2026, laboratory personnel conducted activities under an approved protocol involving pigs and administered
Calcium Chloride. This medication was not approved by the IACUC for this study.
In February 2026, laboratory personnel were conducting activities under an approved protocol involving rabbits that were
to receive cyclosporine at 15mg/kg for 2 weeks (14 days) and then 10mg/kg for two weeks (14 days). However, the
rabbits received the higher dose, 15mg/kg, for approximately 18-19 days, which was 4-5 days longer than what was
approved in the protocol. The protocol deviation was identified as due to a study software error after further investigation.
Although there were no recorded post-procedural complications or medical issues noted for the animals listed above, the
protocol was not followed as written and approved by the IACUC.
Performing activities not covered by IACUC-approved protocols can result in harm or death to the impacted animals. The
regulations state that with respect to activities involving animals, the IACUC, as an agent of the research facility, shall
review and approve, require modifications in (to secure approval), or withhold approval of proposed significant changes
regarding the care and use of animals in ongoing activities.
These incidents were identified by the facility prior to inspection and corrective measures were implemented. To remain
corrected after April 7, 2026

In September 2025, an adverse event was identified involving a pig while undergoing an approved protocol activity.
Laboratory personnel retrieved a pig, tag #1294, from its enclosure for a terminal procedure. Following the procedure,
during the required necropsy, laboratory personnel discovered the wrong pig was inadvertently retrieved for the
procedure. The pig that was used, tag #1294, was enrolled in an approved training protocol. The other pig in the room,
tag #931, is the pig that should have been used for the terminal procedure. An internal investigation determined laboratory
personnel was not adequately trained to correctly identify the pig by the ear tag number and verify the protocol number
the pig was enrolled in. Failure to adequately train laboratory personnel may result in harm or death to animals in their
care.
It is the responsibility of the research facility to ensure that all personnel involved in animal use are adequately trained to
perform their duties. Issues associated with staff training can affect animal health. The facility shall ensure that all
personnel are sufficiently trained to conduct their duties.
Corrected prior to the inspection on April 7, 2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
n

narrative:
2.31(c)(7) 
   
Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee (IACUC). 
Review of IACUC records, in addition to interviews with facility personnel and IACUC members, identified the following 
instances where laboratory staff implemented significant changes to ongoing protocols without prior review and approval 
by the IACUC. 
 
 In February 2026, laboratory personnel conducted activities under an approved protocol involving pigs and administered 
Calcium Chloride. This medication was not approved by the IACUC for this study. 
 
 In February 2026, laboratory personnel were conducting activities under an approved protocol involving rabbits that were 
to receive cyclosporine at 15mg/kg for 2 weeks (14 days) and then 10mg/kg for two weeks (14 days). However, the 
rabbits received the higher dose, 15mg/kg, for approximately 18-19 days, which was 4-5 days longer than what was 
approved in the protocol. The protocol deviation was identified as due to a study software error after further investigation.  
 
Although there were no recorded post-procedural complications or medical issues noted for the animals listed above, the 
protocol was not followed as written and approved by the IACUC. 
 
Performing activities not covered by IACUC-approved protocols can result in harm or death to the impacted animals. The 
regulations state that with respect to activities involving animals, the IACUC, as an agent of the research facility, shall 
review and approve, require modifications in (to secure approval), or withhold approval of proposed significant changes 
regarding the care and use of animals in ongoing activities. 
 
These incidents were identified by the facility prior to inspection and corrective measures were implemented. To remain 
corrected after April 7, 2026 
2.32(c)  Critical
   
Personnel qualifications. 
In September 2025, an adverse event was identified involving a pig while undergoing an approved protocol activity. 
Laboratory personnel retrieved a pig, tag #1294, from its enclosure for a terminal procedure. Following the procedure,

during the required necropsy, laboratory personnel discovered the wrong pig was inadvertently retrieved for the 
procedure. The pig that was used, tag #1294, was enrolled in an approved training protocol. The other pig in the room, 
tag #931, is the pig that should have been used for the terminal procedure. An internal investigation determined laboratory 
personnel was not adequately trained to correctly identify the pig by the ear tag number and verify the protocol number 
the pig was enrolled in. Failure to adequately train laboratory personnel may result in harm or death to animals in their 
care. 
 
It is the responsibility of the research facility to ensure that all personnel involved in animal use are adequately trained to 
perform their duties. Issues associated with staff training can affect animal health. The facility shall ensure that all 
personnel are sufficiently trained to conduct their duties. 
 
Corrected prior to the inspection on April 7, 2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with facility representatives.
